Q1: My study routine was pretty scattered, honestly. I work part-time and have a lot going on, so I couldn't stick to a perfect schedule. I'd usually try to carve out time on weekends and a couple evenings during the week, maybe around 11 hours total. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd try to actually do things rather than just read—practice problems, building things out, that kind of approach. It wasn't elegant, but I made it work with what I had.
Q2: It definitely varied based on deadlines and what was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be consistent, hitting the books Tuesday and Thursday nights after work. But then a deadline would come up or something would happen at home, and everything would shift. I'd end up cramming more hours into the weekend before something was due. At 29, you learn to be flexible because life doesn't care about your study schedule.
Q3: Usually it was checking if something was due or if there was new content posted. I'd also log in when I got notification emails about assignments or discussion posts. Sometimes I'd hop on just to see if anyone responded to something I posted in the forums. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more often because I wanted to see my progress and earn those badges—it made it feel less like a chore.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks the gamified elements kind of hooked me into checking in more regularly. I'd log in to see my points and how I was doing compared to where I needed to be. It became almost like checking social media—just a quick look to see what's new. That routine helped me stay connected to the course even when I was stressed or busy with other things.
Q5: The video lectures were gold for me—I watched over 100 minutes worth because I could pause, rewind, and really work through concepts at my own pace. The discussion forums were also helpful because I could bounce ideas off other students and see different perspectives. The gamified tasks made completing things feel rewarding, like I was actually accomplishing something tangible. As a kinesthetic learner, anything I could interact with stuck better than just reading.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't complete a lot of the assignments—my completion rate was pretty low. Some of the reading materials I'd skim or skip entirely if they seemed too theoretical. When you're working with limited resources and time, you have to prioritize what's going to help you pass. If something felt like busywork or wasn't directly connected to the exam, it often got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to the bigger picture or the exam. The gamified tasks were great because even small activities felt meaningful when I could see points adding up. I also valued anything hands-on where I could practice skills rather than just absorb information passively. If I couldn't see the purpose or payoff, it was hard to justify spending my limited time on it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I often couldn't afford. Required ones got priority because they directly affected my grade. But the gamification blurred that line a bit—some optional stuff gave me points or badges, which made me more likely to do them. Still, when push came to shove and time was tight, optional meant 'maybe never' unless it seemed really valuable.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download videos and PDFs to study on the bus or during breaks at work where I didn't have reliable internet. I also used a lot of external YouTube tutorials and free resources online to supplement what the course offered. With limited resources, you learn to piece together learning from wherever you can get it. My notebook was full of handwritten notes that never showed up in any system.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was deep into studying but the LMS wouldn't know it. I'd print out materials and work through them with a pen, making notes and diagrams. Or I'd be at work on my break, reviewing flashcards I made from course content. Sometimes I'd spend hours practicing concepts by hand or discussing them with a friend who knew the subject. None of that shows up in any log.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, then download it or take detailed notes so I could review offline. My internet isn't always reliable, and I can't always be at home to study. So I'd grab what I needed in one session and then work with it for days afterward. The LMS would show one view, but that material might get studied five or six more times on paper.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. Work picked up, stress was hitting medium-high levels, and I had some personal stuff going on. There were weeks where I just couldn't muster the energy to engage meaningfully. I'd log in, look at what needed to be done, and feel overwhelmed. The gamification helped pull me back sometimes—seeing that I was close to a milestone gave me a small push to do something rather than nothing.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS became something I'd avoid because it reminded me of more obligations. Competing responsibilities meant I had to triage constantly. Some weeks I'd only do the bare minimum to not fall completely behind. Fatigue especially hit hard—I'd open materials but my brain just wouldn't absorb anything.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have videos playing but I was zoned out, thinking about other problems. Or I'd scroll through readings without really processing the words. My body was there, my eyes were moving, but my mind was somewhere else. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning. Stress does that to you.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed—when you see a big task and you're already tired, it's easier to push it to tomorrow. Sometimes I avoided things because I wasn't confident I could do them well, so I'd wait until I felt more prepared. That preparation time often never came, and suddenly the deadline was there.
Q16: Deadlines were my main driver, honestly. I'd see something was due in a few days and suddenly that became my priority. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates. It's not the healthiest approach, but when you're juggling work, life, and school, deadlines create the urgency that gets things done. The gamified reminders helped me stay aware of what was coming up.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback, it motivated me to keep going. Seeing that my work was on the right track made the effort feel worthwhile. But feedback was sometimes slow, and by the time it came, I'd already moved on mentally. Quick feedback through the gamified system—like immediate point rewards—was more effective at keeping me engaged in the moment.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad. Good because I could fit it around my schedule, bad because it was easy to fall behind without strict checkpoints. Having 16 online courses meant a lot of content to manage. The gamification added structure that helped me pace myself better than I would have otherwise. Clear modules with visible progress helped me see where I stood.
Q19: The gamified elements definitely made engagement easier—they gave me short-term goals and visible progress. But some aspects made it harder, like when assignment instructions were unclear or when there was too much content without guidance on what to prioritize. With limited time and resources, I needed the course to tell me what mattered most. When it didn't, I had to guess, and sometimes I guessed wrong.
Q20: I think that happens when people are trying to figure things out but struggling to produce finished work. You might watch videos multiple times, read discussions, search for help—all that shows as activity. But if you're confused or overwhelmed, turning that into a submitted assignment is another hurdle entirely. Activity doesn't equal understanding, and understanding doesn't automatically mean you can produce something on deadline.
Q21: That's pretty close to my situation. My exam score was decent and I passed, but my LMS engagement looked low. I studied a lot offline—downloaded materials, handwritten notes, external resources. The system only sees what happens inside it. Someone like me, who learns by doing things with my hands and studying on the go, won't show up as highly engaged even when I'm putting in real work.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, whether you're consistent. That's useful for spotting who might be struggling. But it misses the quality of engagement, the offline work, the mental effort. It can't tell if someone watched a video while really thinking or while half-asleep. It definitely can't capture what you learn from a conversation with a classmate or from practicing on your own.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and putting in effort to understand it, not just going through motions to check boxes. It's when you're curious, when you think about course concepts outside of study time, when you push through difficulty because you want to get it. For me, engagement also means finding ways to learn that work for my style, even if they're not the official methods.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied offline, all the external resources I used, or the mental effort I put into understanding concepts while doing other things. It wouldn't capture the resilience it took to keep going when money was tight and stress was high. It also wouldn't show that the gamification actually helped me stay connected when I might have otherwise checked out. My engagement was real, just not always visible.
